text: This is Jeremy Richmond from Mass Eye and Ear and Mass General Hospital. I will be presenting a case of transoral robotic surgery for a large squamous cell carcinoma of the base of tongue. As you can see from the initial assessment, this tumor was filling much of the oropharynx.

The surgeon evaluates the tumor, identifying a narrow stalk along the lingual tonsils. The procedure begins with an incision through the lingual tonsils at the base of the tongue to debulk the lesion.

The surgical field shows the remaining narrow and superficial invasive front of the lesion in the lingual tonsillar tissue, following the removal of the majority of the tumor. The surgeon prepares to proceed with definitive oncologic resection.

The surgeon makes an incision through the lingual tonsil along the glossotonsillar sulcus on the right side, extending anteriorly and inferiorly towards the vallecula and over to the left side. A deeper resection is performed over the invasive portion of the tumor to secure a deep negative margin.
The surgeon makes final cuts through the vallecula with clear visualization of the epiglottis, removing the invasive base of the cancer in one block.
The surgical specimen, removed from the base of the tongue, is placed on the back table and inked for pathology. Frozens are taken intraoperatively to ensure a negative margin.
The post-resection view of the base of the tongue is shown, highlighting the dramatic difference compared to the preoperative state. Fibrin sealant is applied to cover the exposed raw surfaces.
The patient was extubated. He was evaluated by the speech and language pathology service. and discharged home on post-operative day one, tolerating a liquid and soft diet.
Thank you for watching. This is Jeremy Richmond from Massachusetts Eye and Ear.
